reddit.journalism:
So here's some context: 2,500+ US newspapers have closed since 2005, and 213 counties now have literally zero journalists covering them. We're a couple of students who thought about this during a hackathon and built a platform where anyone can submit a news tip — voice memo, photo, written notes, whatever — and it gets turned into a structured article. It's called Port, and it's completely free.

We've got 68 articles across 15 towns so far, mostly places where the paper recently died. Chesterton, Indiana lost the Tribune after 141 years. Laurel, Mississippi lost the Leader-Call after 100. Spencer, Tennessee has tried 9 different newspapers since 1915 and none survived. These aren't hypothetical news deserts — they're real towns where nothing gets written down anymore.

Look, we know AI-generated articles aren't the same as having a real reporter on the ground. We're not pretending otherwise. But for a town that currently has zero coverage, an AI article based on a community member's firsthand account is better than the Facebook post that disappears tomorrow. We also think laid-off local reporters could use something like this to keep covering their communities without needing a publisher behind them.

This is early and rough — hackathon project, not a polished product. We'd genuinely love to hear what journalists think about this approach, including all the ways it could go wrong. We've thought about some of them but probably not all.

https://port.news
reddit.localnews:
We built a free local news site specifically for towns that lost their newspapers, and it already has 68 articles from 15 different communities. The idea is simple: if you live somewhere and know what's happening, you can submit a tip — record a voice memo, snap some photos, jot down notes — and the platform turns it into a proper article.

Some of the towns we're covering: Chesterton, Indiana (Tribune closed after 141 years), Claremont, New Hampshire (Eagle Times suspended operations), Laurel, Mississippi (Leader-Call gone after a century), Spencer, Tennessee (9 newspapers have tried and failed there since 1915). Real articles about real things happening in these places — community meals, local businesses opening, school events, county news.

We should be honest: this is a student/hackathon project, not a newsroom. The articles are AI-assisted based on what community members submit, so they're only as good as the tips people send in. But the alternative in most of these towns is literally nothing. No paper, no reporter, no record of what happens. We figured something is better than nothing.

If you're in a town that lost its paper (or is about to), take a look and maybe submit a tip about something happening near you. And if you think this approach is flawed, we want to hear that too.

https://port.news
reddit.technews:
About 2,500 US newspapers have shut down since 2005, leaving 213 counties with zero journalists. We're students who built an open platform that turns community-submitted tips into local news articles using AI. The pipeline works like this: someone submits a voice memo, photo, or text about something happening in their town. The system transcribes it, generates a structured article, runs an AI review pass (checking facts against source material, flagging unsupported claims, noting missing perspectives), and publishes. The whole thing takes about 3 minutes and costs roughly $0.05 per article.

We've got 68 articles across 15 towns so far — places like Chesterton, Indiana (lost their 141-year-old paper), Spencer, Tennessee (9 newspapers have attempted to serve this town since 1915, all failed), and several towns in Finland where local papers recently closed. The platform is completely free.

The obvious question is quality, and we're not going to pretend AI articles are equivalent to professional journalism. They're not. But for communities that currently have zero coverage — where town council decisions, business openings, and local events just go unrecorded — an AI-structured article based on a resident's firsthand account is a meaningful improvement over nothing. The cost structure is what makes it interesting: serving a town costs under $5/year, so it doesn't need to be a viable business per town to work.

This is a hackathon/student project, so it's rough around the edges. Happy to answer technical questions about the pipeline or hear why this is a terrible idea.

https://port.news

reddit.suomi:
118 suomalaisessa kunnassa on 0-1 toimittajaa, ja monessa paikassa paikallislehti on lopettanut kokonaan. Karkkilan Tienoo lakkautettiin 2022, Turkulainen lopetti 2020, Loviisan Sanomat leikattiin yhteen numeroon viikossa 2024. Rakensimme opiskelijaprojektina ilmaisen alustan johon kuka tahansa voi lahettaa uutisvinkin — aaniviestilla, kuvalla tai tekstilla — ja tekoaly muokkaa siita artikkelin.

Sivustolla on jo 33 artikkelia neljasta kunnasta: Karkkila, Turku, Kemi ja Loviisa. Aiheina on ollut kaikkea pyoraileykampanjoista lounastarjouksiin ja kulttuuriavustuksiin. Ei mitaan maailmaa mullistavaa, mutta juuri sita arkista paikallista tietoa mika katoaa kun lehti lakkautetaan.

Myonnetaan suoraan: tekoaly ei korvaa oikeaa toimittajaa. Mutta kun vaihtoehto on etta kunnassa ei kirjoiteta mitaan mistaan, niin tekoalyartikkeli asukkaan vinkin pohjalta on parempi kuin tyhja sivu. Tama on hackathon-projekti, ei mikaan valmis tuote — palautetta ja kritiikkia otetaan mielellaan vastaan.

https://port.news
